chapters.title: Not all jobs are the same
chapters.text:
Anthropologist David Graeber spent countless hours pondering the purpose of work, leading to his concept known as bullshit jobs. We will refer to these as BS jobs throughout this summary. According to him, these roles have little or no meaningful impact or contribution to the greater good. They often involve unnecessary bureaucracy, heavy paperwork, or redundant tasks, usually with pay that is nothing to write home about.

To illustrate, suppose Kurt works for a company that provides human resources for a logistics organization. This entity is a subcontractor for an IT firm supporting the German military. But Kurt’s role is far from being called a military job. When the military needs IT deliveries, they go through the IT company, which reads and approves the application, then passes it to the logistics team. They would contract Kurt’s organization for personnel, and Kurt’s boss would send him to run the deliveries. There are thousands, if not millions, of people worldwide who face some variation of Kurt’s situation, working where their absence would barely affect their company’s productivity or output. As you will see, not all BS work involves subcontractors.

When it comes to your career, do not be afraid to take risks. Unapologetically pursue work that gives you meaning. As the lines between necessary work and superfluous employment become increasingly blurred, questions about the true nature of meaningful labor, its impact on individuals, and the broader implications for society have come to the surface. Read on to learn more about BS jobs and how to free yourself and lift your productivity, no matter where you work.

chapters.title: Common misconceptions about BS jobs
chapters.text:
Aside from BS jobs, there are two other common job types. Useful jobs have a clear purpose and significantly add value to people and society, involving tasks that have practical use. Examples include nurses, bus drivers, military and paramilitary positions, musicians, engineers, and developers. Jobs held by selfish individuals include gangsters, criminals, cartels, barons, and slumlords. People in these roles put their selfish interests ahead of the security and welfare of society, promoting exploitation, denial of rights, and objectification.

When discussing BS work, some common misconceptions come up. One is that BS jobs exist only in the public sector, but our hypothetical story about Kurt shows that the private sector is not exempt. BS positions are not always low-skilled or minimal-training roles. They refer to positions without meaning or purpose to society whose removal would have no significant impact. Any job serving a clear purpose or hitting specific goals cannot be considered a BS job.

The second misconception is that administrative positions, especially those filled by women, are BS jobs. This notion is sexist and misunderstands how offices operate. Another misconception comes from Douglas Adams’ novel, The Restaurant at the End of the Universe, where a particular planet decides to rid itself of useless inhabitants, including hairdressers. Yet hairdressing has never been useless, as many people rely on the service.

If you are unsure whether a role is important, ask yourself whether the job serves a clear purpose and whether it can be eliminated without the organization or society being affected. By continuously reflecting on the significance of our roles and seeking work that truly resonates with us, we can find greater fulfillment and lead more productive, meaningful lives in our professional journeys. People are motivated to be productive when they clearly understand how their work makes a difference.

BS jobs can harm mental health and well-being. For example, an employee in a data entry position may feel demoralized and stressed because of the repetitive nature of the work.

chapters.title: Pointless roles show up in several different forms
chapters.text:
David Graeber identified five major BS work categories: flunkies, goons, duct tapers, box tickers, and taskmasters. Graeber built these categories from firsthand testimonies and from analyzing each person’s experience. Let us look at each type in more detail.

Flunkies are primarily designed to cater to the needs and desires of wealthy and influential individuals, aiming to create an impressive image for those they serve. For example, in the Victorian era, footmen dressed in elaborate uniforms were hired by affluent English households to chase carriages on foot, raising the status of their employers. Their tasks were to check for bumps on the road and clear the way for the carriage. Contemporary examples may include office receptionists or cold callers for brokers, whose main purpose is to make their superiors feel valued and supported.

Goons include aggressive jobs such as positions in the national armed forces, lobbyists, and corporate lawyers. While these jobs may serve their intended purpose, they often have a negative effect. Although goons may resemble Mafia hitmen, Graeber classifies the latter as pointless because the workers often feel their work has no social value and would be better off not existing.

Duct tapers are people hired to temporarily fix or ease organizational problems without addressing the root cause. They offer quick but temporary solutions to maintain the illusion of progress. For example, an IT technician may keep patching computer systems without addressing the underlying issue, such as replacing damaged parts or the computers themselves. Sacrificing and solving problems in one go is more productive than chasing temporary fixes.

Box tickers exist primarily to make an organization appear to be doing something it is not. They handle paperwork and bureaucratic tasks that only create the appearance of progress or compliance.

Taskmasters can fall into two types. The first are managers or supervisors who oversee subordinates capable of working independently, making the supervisor’s role meaningless. The second type consists of people who assign meaningless tasks to others. However, this type is tricky because the worker’s perception plays a big role. If the subordinate finds satisfaction in the assignment, it may no longer be considered meaningless. We will explore these nuances in more detail.

chapters.title: BS jobs cause psychological unhappiness
chapters.text:
You might assume getting paid to do nothing would bring joy to anyone, but people with such jobs often experience feelings of worthlessness and depression. Curiously, these employees struggle to articulate the exact reasons behind their inevitable unhappiness, which deepens their misery. The absence of a clear explanation compounds their distress, creating a sense of confusion and discontent.

Let us explore an example. After graduation, Eric secured a job as an interface administrator for a design firm. He soon discovered that the position existed only because of organizational inefficiencies and interpersonal conflicts within the company. As a result, he spent most of his time on menial tasks that contributed little to the actual design work and the firm’s overall success.

Feeling unmotivated, Eric started showing up late, reading novels during work hours, taking lengthy lunch breaks, and leaving early. He even showed up drunk but still got paid. Eric deliberately engaged in unwelcome behaviors to get fired, but to his surprise, the organization rewarded him with a raise because they needed someone to fill that role. This left him feeling trapped until he eventually resigned.

There are many other reasons why BS roles lead to unhappiness. There is a lack of meaningful work, since most people crave meaningful activities and jobs with social value and reputable status, which BS jobs often lack. There is alienation, since BS positions can cause a rift between your career and yourself, draining motivation and belonging. There is devaluation of skills and talents, since BS roles make you perform tasks that do not align with your abilities, leaving your skills dormant and depreciating from a lack of practice. There is a lack of autonomy and control, since BS jobs involve micromanagement, where superiors or supervisors closely watch and control individuals. The lack of autonomy can be demoralizing and make you feel powerless.

There is also social stigma, since people often judge others based on wealth, fame, position, and status. Society values meaningful and high-paying positions that add value to the community, and so they see BS jobs as wasteful and unproductive, leaving jobholders feeling worthless. These factors can vary from person to person because of personal preferences. As David Graeber once put it, in places without clocks, time is measured by actions rather than actions being measured by time.

chapters.title: The human cost of meaningless jobs
chapters.text:
Beyond unhappiness, BS jobs carry other dangers. There is the misery of ambiguity and forced pretense, where these positions often leave workers unsure of their roles and expectations, leading to frustration and dissatisfaction because of the absence of clear rules. There is the misery of lacking a sense of purpose, since BS jobs strip individuals of meaning and impact, leaving them thinking their work is pointless and lacks real value.

There is also the misery of knowing your job causes harm. People in certain roles, like active service and government positions, may know that their jobs harm others. They may be involved in practices that go against their ethics, causing guilt and distress. This misery arises when they realize they are hurting rather than helping the people they are meant to serve.

For example, a therapist named Shihi worked in a community mental health center in the 1990s and 2000s. Her job was to provide therapy to individuals, but she later discovered that many of her clients were not receiving the help they needed and that the company was benefiting more. Most of her clients were funneled into the system after losing their jobs or suffering incarceration. Some came because they needed to prove to welfare offices that they required financial assistance or other subsidies, mostly food and rent, due to their mental illness. Only a fraction of the patients needed therapy.

For Shihi, the worst part of her job was that the NGO exploited people. The organization would encourage therapy patients to visit regularly so they could bill their Medicaid. They did this by sending patients’ medical records to the Social Security office to be reviewed for disability payments. Shihi eventually left because she could not cope with the guilt of knowing the NGO profited from others’ misery. Knowing when to leave a job is crucial for your personal growth and happiness in the long run.

Take a moment to see whether you can recognize any of the above in someone you know.

chapters.title: Why BS roles have grown over time
chapters.text:
In the past, governments intentionally created superfluous roles as part of their comprehensive employment strategies. Numerous economic policies have also aimed at attaining full employment. However, complete job utilization leads to wage increases, which are unwelcome within a capitalist framework. As a result, politicians have pushed for job creation, without emphasizing the purpose of those positions, as long as they keep idle youth off the streets.

Misunderstandings about private sector roles add to the problem. Some claim that these seemingly meaningless jobs are necessary for managing complex processes. Yet this argument has failed to justify such jobs. Some people blame the government for the rise of BS jobs, saying that any misconduct or irregularities in the market result from government mishandling and carelessness, but that is not always true.

For example, the financial industry drives the creation of BS jobs because it builds and handles complicated financial tools like derivatives, which require notable roles to manage them. Yet not all of these roles add value to society. Speculation and trading also play a role. The financial industry leans on high-frequency trading and arbitrage, which involve quick buying and selling for short-term profits. While some positions in trading are needed, an excessive number of specialized jobs are unnecessary. The complexity of the financial industry lies in creating positions that appear necessary but lack real value.

The financial industry also grapples with extensive regulations and compliance requirements, which cause hiring officers to help firms meet standard practices, such as risk analysts and legal professionals. The complexity and abundance of these roles create redundancy in the economy. The financial industry’s handling of fraud and scams, driven by self-interest, has faced much criticism. It has led many to believe that many roles in the financial system exist because of selfish interests rather than the creation of real value. Still, some roles in the financial sector remain indispensable, such as risk management and financial advisory services.

chapters.title: How society has come to view work
chapters.text:
There has been widespread acceptance and little resistance toward the growth of pointless work. Despite the extensive distress it causes, the distinct lack of political and social movements against its creation raises questions about why society has not acted to address the issue. Various factors may contribute to this, which we will now discuss.

Labor and wages are deeply rooted in religious, moral, and economic traditions, such as measuring value by where you work and what you earn. Work as a sacred duty traces back to religious texts and teachings. For example, in the monastic tradition, monks and nuns engage in various forms of labor as a means of spiritual cleansing and discipline in serving God. Their work, whether farming, cleaning, crafting, or copying manuscripts, is part of their spiritual practice and a way to fulfill their religious duties.

The Judeo-Christian tradition views work as both punishment and redemption from sin, evident in Adam and Eve’s banishment from the Garden of Eden and their condemnation to sweat for survival. Following this, work became a moral virtue and a means to fulfill humanity’s duties to God and to society.

Another reason for the weak resistance to BS work is capitalism’s influence on the theory of work. With the rise of capitalism, work became a means of social reform and a virtue in itself. It emphasizes the moral value of hard work and economic success. It also promotes the agenda or belief that diligent work leads to personal and societal development, which many people have embraced. Meaningful work offers chances for growth, accomplishment, and fulfillment.

The next reason relates to the idea that full employment is crucial for becoming a full-fledged adult. Many people grew up believing that having a full-time job is a necessity of adulthood, as if not having one leaves you incomplete. Whether that is true is debatable, but it is a notion many believe in. Since BS roles have become part of societal perception, it will take social awareness to show people the necessity of demanding more from their jobs. Hopefully, this summary has been a step in that direction.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
BS jobs have significant political implications, as they create social divisions and contribute to the exploitation of productive workers while adding little value to society. First, the unemployed or those struggling without work often despise those employed, leading to a division between these two groups. Second, people trapped in BS jobs resent those engaged in meaningful work, feeling undervalued, underpaid, and unappreciated. Much of this resentment is directed at representatives of the political class, because people in BS jobs believe that the political class placed them there and is not taking action to improve their conditions. They also view the political class as corrupt and selfish.

The Universal Basic Income, or UBI, is a potential solution to bridge the gap between BS jobs and meaningful jobs. UBI involves providing a regular income to everyone, regardless of their employment status, easing the pressure and insecurity tied to employment. Equal pay across all levels would also go a long way toward addressing this problem. While UBI is not the only solution, it creates opportunities and promotes equality.

Cultural awareness is essential to address meaningless jobs and promote fulfilling work. Another crucial step is supporting genuine social service organizations that provide valuable roles without harming others. Finally, advocating for change is vital if you find yourself in a work environment that harms the people it claims to serve.

Try this: with everything you have learned in this summary, reflect on how meaningful your job is. How do you feel about your impact, and are you comfortable with the role? If you find your job unfulfilling, take a moment to write down the work you would like to do. Now take action. This might mean dusting off your CV or taking a new course to pursue more meaningful opportunities.
